concepts.title: Back bonding in coordination compounds
concepts.conceptBody:
Important points on Back bonding

Back bonding is more dominant than $\sigma $ bonding in carbonyl complexes.

  • If back bonding increases, electrons in $\pi $ orbitals of CO increases, thus its bond order decreases, consequently bond length increases and vice versa.
  • Shape of anti bonding orbital of $\text{CO}$ is

Therefore back bonding takes place with dry orbital metal which is identical in shape.

concepts.title: d-d transition
concepts.conceptBody:
Colour of Transition metal compounds

A chemical substance looks coloured if it absorbs a portion of visible light $(\lambda -3800-7800{\AA})$ and transmits the rest. For example, if a compound absorbs the yellow-red $(6000-7500~\text{A})$ it appears green (i.e. transmitted light) i.e. the colour is complementary colour. Absorption of radiation excite electron to higher energy state. Therefore, when a substance looks coloured, it means that absorption of visible light and transition of electron must have taken place. Most of the transition metal compounds are coloured. It arises due to the following reasons:

(i) d-d transition

(ii) Charge transfer

The $\mathbf{d-d}$ transition

The five $d$-orbitals of a free ion are degenerate i.e. have equal energy. But in the presence of ligands $\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O},\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}},\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}},{{\text{F}}^{-}} \right.$etc. )the $\text{d}$-orbitals split. It depends on structure of the complex $(\text{Oh}$, $\text{Td},\text{Sq},\text{Pl}$ etc.).

Let us consider $\text{Ti}\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)_{6}^{3+}$. It is an OH complex. The d-orbitals split as,

and single electron occupies ${{t}_{2g}}$ level. When light falls on the complex ${{t}_{2g}}$ electron is excited to ${{e}_{g}}$ level.

This excitation takes place when the compound absorbs radiation corresponding to $\lambda \approx 5000{\AA},$, (grenish-yellow light). Therefore, transmitted wavelength corresponds violet light and the complex looks violet. As electron transition takes place from one group of $d$ orbitals to the other, it is called $d-d$ transition. The $d-d$ transition is shown by transition metal ions of electron configurations, ${{d}^{1}}$, ${{d}^{2}},{{d}^{3}},{{d}^{4}},{{d}^{6}},{{d}^{7}},{{d}^{8}}$, and ${{d}^{9}}$.

Colour of aqua complexes

Colours due to $\text{d}-\text{d}$ transition are faint. Metal ions which have ${{\text{d}}^{0}},~{{\text{d}}^{5}}$ and ${{\text{d}}^{10}}$ configurations are colourless, if at all coloured not due to $\text{d}-\text{d}$ transition.

concepts.title: Charge transfer
concepts.conceptBody:
Colour and charge transfer

Many transition metal ions or compounds such as $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-},\text{CrO}_{4}^{-2},\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{O}$, CdS etc are coloured. They have their $d$-orbitals either vacant or fully filled.

Colour in such compounds is explained by charge transfer mechanism. $\text{MnO}_{4}^{-},\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ agent and ${{\text{O}}^{-2}}$ is reducing agent. As oxidizing agent picks up electrons and reducing agent gives up electrons, electrons moves from ${{\text{O}}^{-2}}$ to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$.

Due to such movement of electrons the ion is coloured.

The colour of brown ring compound $\left[ \text{Fe}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}^{2+}} \right.$, and $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}\left[ \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]$ are also due to charge transfer.

Colours due to charge transfer are intense (deep).

concepts.title: Hydrolysis of transition metal compounds
concepts.conceptBody:
Hydrolysis of Transition metal compounds

Splitting of water molecules by ions is known as hydrolysis. Cation hydrolysis takes place when charge density on the cation is high.

$\text{ Charge density }=\frac{\text{ Charge }}{\text{ Ionic radius }}$

Transition metal ions on the average are small in size and the common oxidation states are +2, +3 etc. Therefore, their charge density is high and they under go hydrolysis.

Thus, soluble salts of transition metals from slightly acidic solution.

\[\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{ Water }\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ Solution, slightly acidic}\text{.}\]

\[\text{FeC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{Water}\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}+3\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\]

Due to such hydrolytic reaction, cations, such as $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}},\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{+6}}$, etc. are not stable in water

$\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{MnO}_{4}^{-}+8{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

$\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{+6}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CrO}_{4}^{-2}+8{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

Covalent transition metal compounds, such as $\text{TiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}},\text{VC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ etc are hydrolysed due to ability of transition metal ion to expand coordination number by the use of empty $\text{d}$-orbitals.

examples.title: Back bonding is involved in which of the organometallic compounds
Which of the following is not true for metal carbonyls

Assertion : $\left[\mathrm{Ti}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ is coloured while $\left[\mathrm{Sc}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ is colourless
Reason : $d-d$ transition is not possible in $\left[\mathrm{Sc}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}$.
Among the following, which is not the $\pi$-bonded organometallic compound
